cards.body: In a virtual indoor experiment, wooden interiors were linked with a lower frontal theta-to-beta EEG ratio than a control room. That pattern was interpreted as less cognitive strain and better sustained attention. The study does not prove that every wood-filled room sharpens the mind, but it suggests that natural material cues can reach beyond taste into measurable brain activity.
cards.expanded_summary: Thirty-six participants experienced a control scene and three nature-inspired indoor conditions: curving forms, nature views, and wooden interiors. The researchers compared EEG markers connected with attention and mental effort, along with affective and performance measures. The standout claim here concerns the wooden condition, where the theta-to-beta ratio was lower than in the control. Since the experiment used simulated rooms and a short exposure, the result is best read as a controlled signal about acute responses, not a complete rule for real buildings.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through EEG patterns, especially frontal theta-to-beta ratio, alongside affective and performance measures collected during virtual-room exposure.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on a controlled virtual-reality study of nature-inspired indoor design. Thirty-six participants experienced one control environment and three experimental environments: curvilinear forms, a nature-view condition, and wooden interiors. Because each participant encountered multiple conditions, the researchers could compare responses within the same person rather than only between different people.

The central measurement was EEG, a method that records electrical activity at the scalp. The paper reports several EEG ratios, including frontal alpha-to-theta ratio, frontal theta-to-beta ratio, and occipital alpha-to-beta ratio. The selected finding focuses on the wooden-interior condition, where the frontal theta-to-beta ratio was significantly lower than in the control condition. The authors interpret this pattern as consistent with reduced cognitive strain and better sustained attention.

In plain English, the wooden virtual room appeared to shift a brain signal associated with mental effort. This does not mean wood directly makes people smarter, nor that the result would automatically appear in every real office, classroom, or home. It means that, in this short simulated exposure, a wood-dominant interior was associated with a measurable EEG difference that the researchers linked to a more efficient attentional state.

The finding is useful because it treats interior materials as experimental variables rather than decorative preferences. The study did not merely ask whether people liked wood, curves, or nature views. It tested whether those environmental cues were accompanied by physiological and psychological differences. That matters for biophilic design, where broad claims are often made about nature-like spaces.

The limits are important. The rooms were virtual, exposure was brief, and the sample was modest. EEG ratios are indirect indicators of cognitive state, not direct proof of comfort, productivity, or long-term health. The strongest conclusion is cautious: wooden visual interiors, in this experimental setup, were associated with an EEG pattern interpreted as lower cognitive strain. It is evidence worth considering, but not a universal design rule.
cards.apa_citation: Cognitive efficiency in VR simulated natural indoor environments examined through EEG and affective responses.

cards.title: Light affects work, but not by one simple rule
cards.body: A review of lighting and task performance found that illuminance, luminance ratio, and color temperature can all matter. Yet the evidence does not collapse into a single best setting. Brighter light often helps up to a point, cooler light may support alertness, and glare can undo the benefit. The practical lesson is tuning, not maximum brightness.
cards.expanded_summary: The review covered studies using cognitive tests, visual tasks, memory, reasoning, reaction time, and workplace productivity measures. It found patterns, but also many reasons for caution: small samples, carryover effects, mixed methods, and different ways of scoring performance. For designers, this means lighting should be matched to task, time of day, visual comfort, and user needs. The same fixture setting may support focused inspection and still be wrong for relaxed creative work, especially if brightness creates glare or discomfort over longer use.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through performance tests, visual tasks, reaction time, memory and reasoning measures, productivity data, and subjective ratings.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card summarizes a review of how lighting conditions relate to task performance in buildings. The paper does not report one new experiment. It organizes prior evidence on illuminance, luminance ratio, correlated color temperature, daylight, outside views, visual acuity, reaction time, memory, reasoning, and productivity. Its value is a careful map of a complicated field rather than a single dramatic result.

The review finds that lighting variables can affect performance, but not by one simple rule. Illuminance may help when tasks require visual detail or sustained cognitive effort. Still, more light is not automatically better. Glare, excessive contrast, poor distribution, or discomfort can reduce or erase the benefit of added brightness. Luminance ratios matter because people must perceive targets against backgrounds, and extreme contrast can make that harder.

Correlated color temperature is another recurring factor. Cooler, higher-CCT light appears to support alertness and sustained attention in some studies. Warmer light may be more suitable where relaxation or creative thinking is the goal. The review therefore treats light as a set of interacting conditions, not a single brightness dial.

The paper is cautious about method. Studies used different samples, durations, rooms, lighting setups, and scoring methods. Some measured speed, others accuracy, and others the amount of work completed. These outcomes are not interchangeable. A condition that increases speed while reducing accuracy is not simply an improvement. The review also notes problems such as small samples, carryover effects in repeated testing, and confounds from daylight, outside views, thermal conditions, or noise.

The practical conclusion is restrained. Lighting is a controllable environmental variable with real implications for work, but it should be specified around tasks and users. Demanding visual work may need adequate illuminance and controlled contrast. Alertness-oriented settings may benefit from cooler, brighter light at suitable times. Glare control remains essential. The review supports thoughtful lighting strategy, not a universal claim that one illuminance level or color temperature improves every kind of performance.
cards.apa_citation: The effect of lighting environment on task performance in buildings - A review.

cards.title: Tree-lined streets showed a calmer attention network
cards.body: In an fMRI experiment, people watched urban videos with no greenery, trees, or trees plus bioswales. Tree exposure was linked with better sustained-attention performance and brain activity consistent with restored top-down attention. Streets without green infrastructure showed more neural vigilance. The bioswale condition produced some restorative neural signs, but did not clearly improve task performance.
cards.expanded_summary: The study tested 43 participants using both brain imaging and the Sustained Attention Response Task. Its value is that it connects urban landscape features to attention measures rather than relying only on preference ratings. The strongest finding favored trees. The mixed result for trees plus bioswales is useful because it warns against treating every green feature as equivalent. Green infrastructure may help attention, but the kind, arrangement, and perceptual clarity of the green element probably matter.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through fMRI, fmri.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on a controlled experiment about urban green infrastructure and attention. Forty-three participants were randomly assigned to watch one of three kinds of urban video: streets with no green infrastructure, streets with trees, or streets with trees and bioswales. The researchers measured attentional functioning with functional magnetic resonance imaging and the Sustained Attention Response Task. This combination matters because it joins a behavioral measure of attention with neural evidence about the systems supporting that attention.

The clearest pattern favored the tree condition. Participants exposed to urban scenes with trees showed improved top-down attentional functioning, with support from both fMRI and task results. In the study's framing, this is evidence for attention restoration: natural elements may allow directed attention to recover after fatigue. The no-green-infrastructure condition moved in the opposite direction. Those participants showed increased neural vigilance and reduced SART performance, suggesting that bare urban settings may require more active monitoring rather than allowing recovery.

The trees-plus-bioswales condition was more complicated. It showed some attention-restorative neural activity, but did not produce significant improvement in SART performance. That mixed result is important. It suggests that green infrastructure should not be treated as one undifferentiated category. Trees may offer a perceptual structure that is especially effective for effortless attention, while the added bioswale condition may have introduced complexity, novelty, or visual demands that changed the result. The input does not allow a firm explanation, so the cautious conclusion is that trees had stronger support than the combined treatment in this experiment.

The selected source claim concerns functional connectivity: anterior cingulate cortex connectivity with a visual region was higher in the no-green-infrastructure group than in tree-related groups. The paper interprets such patterns as part of the neural story of attentional effort and restoration. Still, functional connectivity is not a simple meter of well-being. It is a statistical relation between brain regions during the measured condition.

The scope is limited. The study used videos, not actual walks through streets. The sample was modest. The result concerns short-term exposure and a specific experimental context in Taipei City. Even so, it strengthens the design-relevant idea that street trees are not merely decorative. In this experiment, they were linked to measurable differences in attention and brain function.
cards.apa_citation: Impacts of urban green infrastructure on attentional functioning: insights from an fMRI study.

cards.title: Brief blue light reached working-memory brain circuits
cards.body: In a daytime fMRI study, healthy young adults performed an auditory working-memory task after short exposure to blue or green monochromatic light. Blue light enhanced, or at least preserved, activity in frontal and parietal regions involved in working memory, and in the thalamus. The effect points to nonvisual light pathways, not just ordinary brightness.
cards.expanded_summary: The study matters because the light exposure was brief, about 18 minutes, and the task was cognitive rather than visual. Blue-sensitive melanopsin retinal cells are a plausible pathway, since they respond strongly to short-wavelength light and project into systems regulating arousal and circadian timing. The sample was small and young, so the result should not be generalized to all people or all lighting schemes. It does show that spectral composition can matter during mental work.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through fMRI brain activation during an auditory working-memory task after brief blue or green monochromatic light exposure.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on an fMRI study of nonvisual light effects during a working-memory task. The participants were healthy young adults, with an input sample described as 18 people aged 18 to 29. They performed an auditory working-memory task while the researchers measured brain responses. The light manipulation compared short daytime exposure to monochromatic blue light and monochromatic green light. The exposure lasted about 18 minutes, which is important because the paper argues that light can affect cognitive brain activity over a fairly short time scale.

The biological background is that light affects the brain in more than one way. Rods and cones support conventional vision, but melanopsin-expressing retinal ganglion cells are especially sensitive to short-wavelength blue light, around 470 to 480 nanometers. These cells are part of nonvisual light signaling. They are often discussed in relation to circadian timing and arousal, but this study asked whether they could also modulate brain responses during a complex cognitive task.

The main finding is that blue light differentially affected brain activity compared with green light. During the auditory working-memory task, blue light typically enhanced responses, or prevented declines otherwise observed after green light, in frontal and parietal cortices involved in working memory. It also affected the thalamus, a structure relevant to arousal and cognitive modulation. The result does not mean that participants consciously saw better or that blue light simply looked brighter. The point is that spectral composition, even at equated light exposure, can influence brain systems supporting attention and memory.

For design, the implication is restrained but useful. Daytime environments that demand sustained working memory may be affected by the spectral content of light, not only by illuminance. Control rooms, study spaces, or other high-attention settings may need lighting strategies that consider blue-rich daytime exposure. However, the study does not by itself define a universal office setting. It used monochromatic light, a small young sample, a laboratory task, and brain activation rather than long-term work output.

The cautious conclusion is that brief blue light exposure can alter neural responses during working memory in the measured context. It supports the idea that light is a biological signal as well as a visual aid, but it leaves open questions about dose, timing, individual sensitivity, and real-world application.

cards.title: Bright daytime light helped fatigue, but unevenly
cards.body: In a within-subject experiment, participants experienced 200 lux and 1000 lux light after either a fatigue induction or a control condition. Bright light made people feel less sleepy, more vital, and happier. Some effects were stronger after mental fatigue, while task performance was mixed. The finding is useful precisely because it is not a simple brightness story.
cards.expanded_summary: The study tested 28 participants across 106 sessions, measuring self-reports, task performance, and physiological arousal. Bright light improved subjective alertness and mood, and vigilance improved after a delay. But other tasks showed mixed or even adverse effects. That means bright light may be a practical support for daytime fatigue, especially for sleepiness and vitality, while still needing care around task type, timing, glare, and individual response. The evidence favors adaptive use over a blanket instruction to make workplaces brighter.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through self-report ratings, task performance, vigilance measures, and physiological arousal during 200 lux and 1000 lux lighting sessions.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on a laboratory study of daytime bright light, mental fatigue, and performance. The researchers used a 2 by 2 within-subject design with 28 participants and 106 total sessions. The two lighting conditions were 200 lux and 1000 lux at the eye, with a constant color temperature described in the input as 4000 K. The two antecedent conditions were a fatigue condition and a control condition. In other words, the study asked whether bright light is especially helpful after mental exertion, not only after sleep loss or at night.

The strongest effects were subjective. Participants felt less sleepy, more vital, and happier under bright light. Effects on subjective sleepiness and self-control capacity were stronger when participants had first been mentally fatigued. That is a practical result because many daytime environments are not sleep-deprivation settings. People may be technically awake and still depleted by sustained cognitive work. The study suggests that higher illuminance can influence how awake and capable people feel in that state.

The performance findings were more complicated. Vigilance benefited from bright light, but the benefit emerged after a delay and did not depend strongly on whether participants had been fatigued first. Other tasks showed mixed outcomes, with some even moving in an adverse direction. This is important for interpretation. Bright light should not be treated as a general cognitive enhancer that improves every task immediately. It may help alertness, vitality, and vigilance while producing weaker or inconsistent effects on other forms of performance.

The selected claim notes that the interaction between lighting condition and antecedent condition on vitality was not significant. That means fatigue did not significantly moderate the vitality effect, even though some other subjective measures showed stronger lighting effects under fatigue. This detail keeps the result from being overstated. Bright light had beneficial subjective effects, but not every outcome depended on fatigue status in the same way.

For design, the paper supports a measured claim: brighter daytime light can reduce sleepiness and improve subjective vitality and mood, and may help vigilance after some delay. It does not prove that 1000 lux is always better than 200 lux, nor that bright light should be used without considering glare, comfort, task demands, and timing. The study is best used as evidence for adaptive lighting strategies in mentally demanding daytime settings.
cards.apa_citation: Lighting condition (fatigue vs. control induction) and baseline measurements.

cards.title: Wood smells lifted mood more clearly than physiology
cards.body: A small laboratory study tested pine needle oil, cedar wood oil, alpha-pinene, and thymoquinone while measuring heart rate, finger temperature, skin conductance, and self-reported feelings. The wood-related odors differed in pleasantness, familiarity, and intensity, and they improved feelings such as happiness and contentment. Clear odor-specific physiological effects did not appear.
cards.expanded_summary: The study is useful because it separates subjective experience from physiological measurement. Participants did not merely say whether a smell was pleasant; their autonomic responses were monitored during exposure. The result was mixed: mood reports shifted more clearly than heart rate, temperature, or skin conductance by odor type. Some physiological measures correlated with feeling relaxed or happy, but the evidence does not show a simple body response for each wood scent. It points to mood effects that are real enough to study, but not yet mechanically tidy.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through odor ratings, self-reported mood, heart rate, finger temperature, and skin conductance during exposure to wood-related scents.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on an empirical study of wood odors as possible ambient scents. Twenty healthy women participated in a controlled laboratory experiment. They smelled pine needles oil, cedar wood oil, alpha-pinene, and thymoquinone, then evaluated the odor percept and their own subjective feelings. The researchers also monitored heart rate, finger temperature, and skin conductance throughout the experiment. The design therefore included both self-report and physiological measures.

The central result is mixed in a useful way. The odors were clearly distinguishable in how participants rated them. They differed in pleasantness, familiarity, and intensity. Across odors, participants reported enhanced feelings of happiness and contentment. That supports the idea that wood-related odors can affect subjective mood and may be relevant for ambient scent design. However, the study did not find clear odor-specific physiological effects. Heart rate, finger temperature, and skin conductance did not produce a simple pattern in which each odor generated a distinct bodily signature.

This distinction matters. It would be tempting to say that wood odors reliably relax the body. The input does not support that broad claim. A better reading is that these odors influenced reported mood, while physiological responses were weaker, less odor-specific, or more variable. Some physiological measures did correlate with self-reports of happy and relaxed feelings and with odor intensity ratings. That suggests that subjective experience and autonomic state are not unrelated, but it does not establish a strong causal map from each scent to each physiological outcome.

The selected source claim points to non-significant Friedman tests for several subjective evaluation items, with some significant Wilcoxon differences. This fits the broader pattern: some rating differences were present, but the evidence was not uniformly strong across all measures. The study also used a small and demographically narrow sample, which limits generalization. Odor responses can depend on culture, memory, prior associations, sensitivity, concentration, and context.

For design, the cautious implication is that ambient wood odors may influence mood and perceived atmosphere, but should not be specified as if they guarantee physiological relaxation. They are plausible tools for multisensory environmental design, especially where pleasantness and natural associations matter. Further work would need larger samples, more varied populations, controlled concentrations, and real-room exposures to determine whether wood odors produce reliable stress recovery outside the laboratory.
cards.apa_citation: Out of the woods: psychophysiological investigations on wood odors to estimate their suitability as ambient scents.

cards.title: Crowding stressed walkers when it slowed them down
cards.body: Across studies of pedestrian density, walking speed, electrodermal activity, and subjective stress, crowding alone did not reliably raise physiological arousal. The more important condition was being crowded while movement was constrained. When density reduced walking speed, arousal increased. The unpleasantness of proximity was real, but the bodily stress signal depended on what people could do.
cards.expanded_summary: This distinction matters for stations, corridors, shopping arcades, and other public movement systems. A dense crowd waiting in place is not the same as a dense crowd trying to move. The research supports part of the pedestrian level-of-service idea, but with a boundary: stress rises most clearly when density interferes with walking. Design should therefore protect movement choice, not merely count bodies per square meter. Bottlenecks, forced slowing, and loss of pace may matter more than density alone.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through electrodermal activity, skin conductance responses, walking speed, pedestrian density, and subjective stress or unpleasantness ratings.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on a multi-study investigation of pedestrian density, walking speed, and psychological stress. The work examined crowded situations using both subjective ratings and objective physiological measures, especially electrodermal activity. The broader practical question was whether the engineering idea of pedestrian level of service aligns with human stress. Pedestrian level of service assumes that higher density and reduced movement quality lower the experience of a space. This research asks when that assumption appears in the body, not only in opinion.

The key finding is conditional. Pedestrian density by itself did not reliably produce objective physiological stress across all settings. Walking speed by itself also did not always produce the effect when considered separately. The stronger pattern appeared when the two were combined: density that reduced walking speed led to increased physiological arousal. In plain terms, a crowd becomes more stressful when it interferes with intended movement. Being near other people may be unpleasant, but being near them while losing control over pace is a different condition.

The studies included waiting and walking situations. Studies of standing or waiting in dense settings found that people could rate proximity as unpleasant without always showing the same physiological stress response. Walking studies added the crucial dynamic component. When participants had to move through density, and especially when density constrained speed, electrodermal measures such as skin conductance level and non-specific skin conductance responses became more relevant indicators of sympathetic activation.

The selected claim notes that participants in two groups found physical proximity equally unpleasant. That supports a subtle reading. Subjective unpleasantness can be similar across conditions even when physiological arousal differs, or physiological arousal can depend on movement constraints rather than proximity alone. Design should therefore avoid reducing crowd experience to a single density number. The same density may have different consequences in a waiting area, a corridor, a platform, or a bottleneck.

The evidence is moderate and context-bound. The research spans several studies and conditions, which strengthens the pattern, but the settings are still experimental or quasi-naturalistic. Real public spaces add urgency, luggage, noise, social norms, wayfinding, heat, and safety concerns. The practical conclusion is not that all crowding causes stress. It is that crowding becomes physiologically more consequential when it removes freedom of movement and slows pedestrians against their intention.
cards.apa_citation: The Relationship between Pedestrian Density, Walking Speed and Psychological Stress: Examining Physiological Arousal in Crowded Situations.

cards.title: Curves may reach awareness faster than categories
cards.body: In continuous flash suppression experiments, faces broke through into awareness faster than objects. But the expected face-specific neural signal was absent. A later test suggested why: curvilinear objects broke through just as quickly as faces and faster than rectilinear objects. The advantage may come from curved contours, not from face identity itself.
cards.expanded_summary: The study is relevant beyond face perception because it separates high-level meaning from mid-level shape. If curved forms gain faster access to awareness under suppression, some preferences for faces or rounded objects may reflect visual processing fluency rather than social category alone. For architecture and product design, the result should be used carefully: these were grayscale images in a perception experiment, not rooms. Still, the curvature mechanism is a useful clue for why rounded forms can feel more immediately available to perception.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through continuous flash suppression breakthrough times and steady-state visually evoked potential recordings.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on three studies using continuous flash suppression, behavioral breakthrough time, and steady-state visually evoked potentials. Continuous flash suppression presents an image to one eye while a dynamic mask shown to the other eye keeps the image outside conscious awareness for a period of time. The key behavioral measure is breakthrough time: how long it takes before the hidden image becomes consciously detectable.

The first result fits a familiar idea in perception. Faces broke through suppression faster than non-face objects. Since faces are socially important, it is tempting to interpret this as evidence that the brain detects face category outside awareness. But the neural measure complicated that story. Under suppression, faces did not evoke a measurable face-selective steady-state visually evoked potential. In other words, the behavioral advantage appeared without the expected neural evidence for nonconscious face-specific cortical processing.

The third study tested a different explanation: curvilinearity. The researchers compared faces, curvilinear objects, rectilinear objects, and mixed objects. Curvilinear objects broke through suppression faster than rectilinear objects, and there was no meaningful difference between faces and curvilinear objects. This supports the interpretation that the faster breakthrough for faces may be driven by mid-level visual features, especially curved contours, rather than by high-level face category membership.

The selected claim concerns post-hoc tests showing that object breakthrough time was significantly slower than for neutral faces in one comparison, while other comparisons were less clear. The broader evidence from the set of studies is that the face advantage exists behaviorally, but its cause may not be face identity. This is an important correction because it reduces an attractive but overly broad conclusion. The visual system may prioritize certain shapes before it identifies what those shapes mean.

For design, the implication is suggestive, not definitive. Curved contours in objects or spaces may be processed more fluently or gain attention more readily than angular forms. That could help explain why rounded forms sometimes feel more approachable or visually engaging. But the experiments used images, not built spaces; sample sizes were modest; and one input notes preprint status and limits in stimulus design. The strongest claim is perceptual: under these laboratory conditions, curvature helped explain faster access to awareness better than face category alone.
cards.apa_citation: Faces under continuous flash suppression capture attention faster than objects, but without a face evoked steady-state visual potential: Is curvilinearity responsible for the behavioral effect?.

cards.title: Biophilic design is promising, but still a framework
cards.body: This paper argues that human brains evolved in natural environments while modern life has moved largely into cities. It presents biophilic design as a possible way to reduce urban stress and support health, creativity, and well-being. The claim is broad and useful, but it is mainly a synthesis and framework, not a single decisive experiment.
cards.expanded_summary: The article connects urbanization, mental health concerns, natural settings, and design strategies such as greenspace and close-to-nature features. Its strength is integration: it gathers reasons why cities might burden stress systems and why nature-like environments may help. Its limit is the same as its scale. The paper can guide hypotheses and design thinking, but specific claims about dose, feature type, population, and causal effect still need targeted empirical testing before they become dependable rules for buildings or cities.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through synthesized evidence from rating tasks, EEG experiments, virtual-reality experiments, and broader studies of nature exposure and well-being.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on a synthesis-oriented article about biophilic design, nature, health, creativity, and well-being. The paper starts from a broad premise: the human brain evolved in adaptive response to natural environments, while most contemporary humans increasingly live in cities. The article links urbanization with changes in stress exposure, mental health risk, and the structure of everyday sensory environments. It then presents biophilic design as a possible approach for making urban settings more supportive.

The article is not best read as a single experiment with one sample, one manipulation, and one outcome. The input describes it as theoretical, with figures but no tables, and with a focus on conceptual integration. It draws together ideas about greenspace, close-to-nature characteristics, stress, mental health, creativity, and sustainable living. That makes its contribution different from a trial showing that one plant, one window view, or one wall material caused one measured effect. Its contribution is to argue that environmental design should treat nature contact as a serious health-relevant variable.

The selected source excerpt concerns city form and navigation differences across countries, including Argentina and Romania. That detail fits the article's broader theme: urban environments are not neutral containers. Their form, regularity, navigational structure, and access to natural features may shape cognition and well-being. However, that excerpt alone should not be inflated into a general law about all cities or all people. It is one piece in a larger framework about how built form and nature exposure might matter.

For design, the article supports a direction rather than a precise specification. It encourages designers and planners to consider greenspace, natural materials, views, complexity, and other biophilic features as possible protective factors in urban life. But the evidence remains uneven across features and outcomes. Some parts of the nature-and-health literature are stronger than others, and specific mechanisms still require testing.

The scope and uncertainty are therefore central. This paper is useful for framing why biophilic design might matter at urban scale and why it belongs in conversations about public health and sustainability. It should not be used to claim that any nature-themed element automatically improves health, creativity, or well-being. It is a conceptual bridge from evolutionary and environmental reasoning to design practice, with many empirical details still open.
cards.apa_citation: The Potential of Biophilic Design and Nature to Improve Health, Creativity and Well-Being.

cards.title: Office well-being evidence is strongest for climate and privacy
cards.body: A systematic review of office studies found that perceived indoor conditions relate to well-being, satisfaction, productivity, and sometimes cognitive performance. The evidence is not evenly distributed. Indoor climate, space, furniture, privacy, and natural visual quality are studied more often and supported more consistently than decoration or maintenance. Perception itself becomes a design variable.
cards.expanded_summary: The review screened more than fourteen thousand records and included 55 workplace studies. It grouped eleven perceived environmental domains into five larger factors, then mapped their links to worker outcomes. The result is an evidence map, not a pooled effect-size formula. It shows where designers can speak with more confidence and where research remains thin, especially for cognitive performance and less-studied environmental features. Its caution is useful: worker experience is measurable, but the literature is still uneven in what it can explain.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Rating Task.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on a systematic review of perceived indoor environmental characteristics in workplaces. The review examined how office workers' perceptions of their environment relate to mental well-being, cognitive performance, productivity, and satisfaction. It was not a new intervention study. The authors searched six databases, screened 14,173 records, and included 55 office-workplace studies. They grouped eleven perceived environmental domains into five broader factors: visual quality of naturalness, visual quality of decoration, space, furniture and privacy, indoor climate, and environmental maintenance.

The central finding is that the evidence is uneven. Indoor climate, space, furniture, privacy, and visual naturalness have been studied broadly, and many studies support links between these perceived conditions and outcomes such as well-being, productivity, and satisfaction. Visual decoration and environmental maintenance received less attention, so the review cannot support equally confident claims about them. Cognitive performance was especially inconsistent across the literature. That inconsistency may reflect differences in tasks, measures, office settings, and study designs.

The selected source claim notes that some studies used physiological instruments, including salivary cortisol concentration, heart rate, skin conductance level, and skin conductance response, to measure occupants' stress levels. This detail is important because the review is not limited to simple preference surveys. Still, much of the literature relies on self-report and cross-sectional associations. That means causal interpretation remains limited. Workers who feel stressed may perceive their offices more negatively, and poor environments may also contribute to stress. Both directions are plausible unless the design can separate them.

For design, the review supports a disciplined approach. Perceived indoor conditions are not trivial; they are part of how occupants experience work. Climate comfort, privacy, furniture, spatial arrangement, and natural visual qualities deserve attention because they appear repeatedly in the evidence base. At the same time, designers should not pretend that all environmental features have the same level of support. Some are better studied than others.

The review's practical value lies in evidence mapping. It helps identify stronger areas for decision-making and weaker areas for future experiments. It does not provide one universal prescription for the ideal office. The cautious conclusion is that perceived workplace environments are associated with meaningful outcomes, but better randomized and quasi-experimental studies are needed to test causality, dose, and transfer across office types.
cards.apa_citation: Association between perceived indoor environmental characteristics and occupants' mental well-being, cognitive performance, productivity, satisfaction in workplaces: A systematic review.

cards.title: Dimmer Classrooms May Help Memory Work Harder
cards.body: Bright classrooms are often treated as automatically better for learning, but this study points in a more complicated direction. In virtual classroom settings, lower illuminance was linked with stronger memory performance and different physiological signals. The finding does not mean all classrooms should be dim. It does suggest that light level can become part of the learning system, not just a visibility setting.
cards.expanded_summary: The study exposed participants to immersive classroom environments with different illuminance levels, then measured memory task performance alongside neurophysiological responses. The notable result was that lower illuminance, especially around 100 lux in this setup, appeared more favorable for memory than brighter conditions. Because the experiment used virtual environments and a specific memory task, the result should be read as a controlled clue, not a universal lighting rule. It asks designers to test learning spaces against actual cognitive response, rather than assuming that more light always means better learning.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through EEG, HRV.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is about a controlled neuroarchitecture study of classroom illuminance, memory, and physiological response. Participants were exposed to immersive virtual environments representing university classrooms with different light levels. The study then examined psychological measures, including presence and memory performance, along with neurophysiological measures such as EEG, HRV, and EDA. The central finding is that lower illuminance, especially a 100 lux condition, was associated with better memory performance and changes in sympathetic or neurophysiological activation in this experimental setting. The input also reports that higher illuminance was associated with lower EDA-phasic values.

The important scientific point is not simply that dim light is good and bright light is bad. That would be too strong. The study instead suggests that illuminance can alter the cognitive and physiological state in which a student performs a memory task. Memory is not only a property of the student's mind; it is measured while the student is inside a sensory environment. If the environment changes arousal, attention, comfort, or neural activation, memory performance may change as well.

The evidence is moderate because the design connects a specific environmental variable to measurable outcomes, but the result remains bounded. The input mentions 142 participants overall and 40 in the main memory-task phase, and the setting was a laboratory experiment using virtual classroom scenes. That makes the study useful for isolating lighting effects, but it also means the findings may not transfer directly to every real classroom, every age group, every subject, or every learning task. The exposure window, the task type, and the virtual scene all matter. A short memory task in an immersive simulation cannot settle how lighting should be set for a full school day, a design studio, or a lecture hall.

For design, the result argues against treating illuminance as a neutral background parameter. It should be considered a controllable design variable with possible effects on memory and physiological response. A cautious practical lesson is that learning spaces may benefit from lighting scenarios tested against the actual use of the room. A reading task, memory task, discussion, and examination may not all require the same luminous condition. The study supports further testing of adaptive classroom lighting, especially where learning performance and occupant physiology can be measured together.
cards.apa_citation: The effects of illuminance on students' memory. A neuroarchitecture study.

cards.title: Green Buildings Aim at Health, Not Just Energy
cards.body: Green building is often presented as an energy story, but this review treats it as a health story too. Efficient lighting, passive design, ventilation, insulation, materials, and biophilic elements can all shape indoor environmental quality. The claim is synthetic rather than experimental: healthier buildings are made by coordinating environmental systems, not by adding one green feature after the fact.
cards.expanded_summary: The review gathers literature on sustainable architecture, indoor environmental quality, occupant health, and energy efficiency. Its main value is in joining concerns that are often separated: reducing environmental impact while improving conditions for people indoors. Because it is a review, it does not provide one decisive effect size for one design feature. Instead, it frames building location, site selection, ventilation, insulation, lighting, materials, and nature-related design as interacting parts of a healthier indoor environment. The evidence is therefore broad, useful, and necessarily cautious.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Narrative Review.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card summarizes a narrative review on green building strategies, indoor environmental quality, energy efficiency, and health. The input describes a review concerned with passive design, biophilic elements, and optimization of indoor environmental quality. It argues that green buildings can respond to energy and environmental pressures while also creating healthier indoor environments. The source claim specifically notes that high-efficiency lighting and appliances can reduce energy consumption while maintaining comfortable indoor conditions.

The science here is mainly integrative. The paper does not report a single experiment in which one room variable was changed and one health outcome was measured. Instead, it synthesizes research across building-related factors such as location, site selection, ventilation, insulation, lighting, appliances, and materials. These factors can affect indoor environmental quality, which in turn may affect comfort, wellbeing, and health. Biophilic design is part of that discussion, but the review is broader than plants or views alone.

The evidence strength should be read as moderate because review evidence can clarify patterns across a field, but it is not the same as a controlled causal test. The input says a systematic approach was used to identify relevant studies, including searches in PubMed, Web of Science, Scopus, and Google Scholar. That improves the credibility of the synthesis. Still, the paper's contribution is a literature-based argument rather than a single measured intervention with a uniform sample, dose, and outcome.

For designers, the practical lesson is that health and energy are not separate domains. A building can save energy and still fail occupants if its indoor conditions are poor. Conversely, a building can support wellbeing while also reducing environmental burden if its systems are coordinated carefully. Efficient lighting and appliances matter, but so do ventilation, insulation, materials, daylight, and nature-related design. The review encourages teams to consider these features as an integrated environmental package.

The uncertainty lies in the details. Which combination works best depends on climate, building type, occupants, maintenance, and measurement methods. The review supports green building as a health-promoting design direction, but it does not settle exact thresholds, doses, or universal prescriptions. Later empirical studies would need to test boundary conditions and compare specific design strategies in real occupied buildings.
cards.apa_citation: Green building design strategies (passive design, biophilic elements, IEQ optimization).

cards.title: Indoor Nature Can Speed Recovery After Stress
cards.body: A plant-filled or nature-facing office may do more than look pleasant after a hard moment. In this virtual reality experiment, people recovered from stress more favorably in biophilic office environments than in a non-biophilic office. The strongest effects appeared early in the six-minute recovery period. The study makes nature indoors a testable recovery condition, not mere decoration.
cards.expanded_summary: Participants were stressed first, then assigned to recover in one of four virtual offices: a baseline office, an office with indoor greenery, an office with an outdoor view, or a combined biophilic condition. The researchers measured anxiety along with heart rate variability, heart rate, skin conductance, and blood pressure. Results consistently favored biophilic environments for stress and anxiety recovery, though the pattern differed by feature and outcome. The evidence is strongest for short-term recovery in a controlled simulated office, not for every real workplace or hospital room.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through heart rate, skin conductance.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on a between-subjects virtual reality experiment testing whether indoor biophilic environments help people recover from stress and anxiety. The study used 100 participants and four virtual office conditions: a non-biophilic baseline office, an office with indoor green elements, an office with an outdoor view, and a combined biophilic condition. Participants completed stressor tasks and then spent a six-minute recovery period in one of the virtual offices.

The study measured both physiological and psychological responses. Physiological indicators included heart rate variability, heart rate, skin conductance level, and blood pressure. Anxiety was measured with a short version of the State-Trait Anxiety Inventory. This is important because the finding is not only about preference or visual appeal. The experiment asked whether the body and self-reported anxiety recover differently when the surrounding office contains nature-related features.

The input reports that participants in biophilic indoor environments had consistently better recovery responses than those in the non-biophilic environment. The effects on physiological responses were immediate, with larger impacts during the first four minutes of the six-minute recovery process. The three biophilic conditions did not all have identical effects, which matters for practice. A view, indoor greenery, and a combined condition should not be assumed to work in the same way or with the same strength.

The evidence is moderate and useful. Random assignment and a controlled VR setup make the comparison cleaner than many real-world studies, because the researchers can hold much of the environment constant while changing nature-related features. At the same time, VR is not the same as a real office, clinic, or home. The recovery window was short, the sample was specific, and the stressor was experimental. The result should therefore support design direction rather than a universal claim.

For design, the main implication is that biophilic elements can be treated as recovery-supportive environmental inputs. They may matter especially after stress has already occurred. This is stronger than saying people like plants. The study suggests that greenery and views can influence measurable recovery processes. Designers of offices, healthcare spaces, waiting areas, and other high-stress interiors should consider testing biophilic features against stress and anxiety outcomes, while keeping claims tied to context, exposure time, and the exact features used.
cards.apa_citation: Jie Yin et al. (). Environment International Effects of biophilic indoor environment on stress and anxiety recovery: A between-subjects experiment in virtual reality. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.envint.2019.105427

cards.title: Wood Interiors May Quiet Some Stress Signals
cards.body: Wood indoors is easy to describe as warm or natural, but this review asks a harder question: does visual exposure to wood change stress indicators? Across nine studies with 386 participants, longer wood exposure generally aligned with better affective states and lower physiological arousal. The pattern is promising, but not clean enough to treat wood as a guaranteed stress treatment.
cards.expanded_summary: The review examined real or simulated visual contact with wooden indoor surfaces compared with non-wooden surfaces. Outcomes included affective, physiological, and cognitive performance measures. Some studies suggested improved mood or reduced arousal in wooden settings, and one claim notes lower blood pressure in preferred settings, though that explanation is described as weak. The careful conclusion is that wood exposure may help certain stress indicators under some conditions. The research base needs stronger methods, clearer comparisons, and more consistent exposure protocols.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Questionnaire, Rating Task.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card summarizes a review of visual exposure to wooden indoor surfaces and indicators of human stress. The review included nine studies with 386 participants in total. It examined whether real or virtual contact with real or imitated wooden surfaces affected affective outcomes, physiological outcomes, or cognitive performance when compared with non-wooden surfaces. The central conclusion is cautious: current evidence suggests that visual wood exposure may improve some stress-related indicators, but the findings are not entirely clear-cut and additional research is needed.

The distinction between visual exposure and other forms of wood contact matters. The review focused on what people see in indoor wood surfaces, not necessarily on touch, smell, acoustics, or structural use of wood. Some included studies with longer exposure times to wood generally observed improved affective states and decreased physiological arousal. That suggests wood may shape indoor experience beyond style or cultural association. But the evidence does not support an unlimited claim that any wood in any room will reduce stress.

The source claim notes that subjects' blood pressure tended to be lower in settings they liked, which could suggest that decreases in blood pressure reflected a positive outcome. However, the input explicitly says the evidence behind this explanation is weak. This is a useful warning. A room may reduce arousal because of material properties, preference, familiarity, expectation, lighting, or other features. If those influences are not separated carefully, it becomes difficult to know what wood itself is doing.

The strength of this paper lies in synthesis and methodological criticism. It gathers a small literature and reads it in light of design relevance, but it also identifies shortcomings. The included studies varied in exposure time, population, setting, comparison surface, and outcome measures. Some evidence came from laboratory contexts, including questionnaire-based measures. Those differences limit how confidently the results can be combined.

For practice, the sound conclusion is that wood should be considered a plausible environmental variable for stress-related design, not a proven universal remedy. Designers may reasonably test wooden surfaces in settings where calm, warmth, or lower arousal is desired, especially if the exposure is long enough to matter. But decisions should be evaluated with both subjective and physiological measures when possible. The review supports further research with better-controlled materials, clearer reporting, and more naturalistic indoor settings.
cards.apa_citation: Effects of visual exposure to.

cards.title: Light Changes Feel Faster Than Bodies Respond
cards.body: When lighting shifts suddenly, people notice at once. In this experiment, transitions between warm dim light and cool bright light quickly changed perceived brightness, color, comfort, alertness, and mood. But physiological measures such as heart rate, heart rate variability, skin conductance, and skin temperature showed little immediate response. The experience of light moved faster than many body signals.
cards.expanded_summary: Thirty-eight healthy adults completed four controlled sessions with different lighting trajectories: warm dim to cool bright, cool bright to warm dim, constant warm dim, and constant cool bright. Subjective appraisals changed quickly and often persisted, while comfort and acceptance showed adaptation over roughly 15 to 30 minutes. Performance and physiological arousal were less responsive than subjective experience. The finding matters for dynamic lighting: abrupt changes may make people feel more alert, yet they can also produce a temporary comfort cost.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through HRV, Heart rate, skin conductance.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is about a controlled within-subjects experiment on abrupt lighting transitions and measures of alertness, arousal, comfort, and thermoregulation. Thirty-eight healthy participants completed four separate 90-minute sessions. The lighting conditions contrasted warm dim lighting with cool bright lighting. Some sessions included transitions from one condition to the other, while others held lighting constant.

The study measured several kinds of response. Subjective measures included perceived light intensity, perceived color temperature, visual comfort, visual acceptance, alertness, and mood dimensions such as calmness, happiness, and vitality. Objective or physiological measures included psychomotor vigilance performance, heart rate, heart rate variability, skin conductance level, skin temperature, and distal-to-proximate skin temperature gradient. The source claim specifically states that mean skin temperature and DPG were not significantly affected by the lighting transitions.

The main pattern is that subjective experience responded more clearly than many physiological indicators. People perceived the change in brightness and color temperature immediately. Alertness and vitality also showed immediate and persistent response. Visual comfort and acceptance were more complicated: transitions, especially toward cool bright light, could produce a temporary dip that recovered within about 15 to 30 minutes. In contrast, physiological measures such as heart rate, HRV, skin conductance, and skin temperature showed little rapid transition effect, apart from a gradual skin conductance increase in constant cool bright lighting described in the input as a possible slow activating effect.

This evidence is useful because it separates different meanings of response. A lighting change can feel powerful without producing a matching rapid physiological shift in every measured system. That matters for design claims. Dynamic lighting may help occupants feel more alert, but designers should not assume that abrupt lighting changes automatically create broad physiological activation during daytime exposure.

The limitations are important. The experiment took place in controlled daytime sessions, and the input notes that the findings may not generalize to evening or nighttime, when light can affect physiology differently. The lighting contrast was strong, so smaller everyday adjustments might produce weaker effects. The sample was modest, and the 90-minute session may miss longer adaptation. For practice, the prudent lesson is to use dynamic lighting carefully: transitions can be effective perceptually, but ramp speed, direction, comfort, and timing all matter.
cards.apa_citation: M.E. Kompier, K.C.H.J. Smolders, W.D. van Marken Lichtenbelt, Y.A.W. de Kort (2020). Effects of light transitions on measures of alertness, arousal and comfort. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physbeh.2020.112999

cards.title: Facades Help Decide How Wide Streets Sound
cards.body: A city square is not only heard from the traffic lane. It is also heard from the walls around it. This study used acoustic simulation and listening tests to show that facade absorption and scattering can change how wide an urban space seems to listeners. The effect depended on listening position, which means urban acoustics is partly a matter of where the body stands.
cards.expanded_summary: The researchers modeled a small square in Turin and varied facade materials through absorption and scattering coefficients. They then used auralized sound scenes in listening tests with 31 participants. The results indicated that facade absorption influenced perceived spatial wideness, and statistical tests supported differences for separate listener positions. A regression analysis linked perception to objective acoustic parameters, but the relation was strongly position-dependent. The finding is practical but bounded: it comes from one simulated square and should be tested in more real urban settings.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Survey, Questionnaire.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card concerns an empirical study of facade design, acoustic characteristics, and auditory perception in urban space. The study used ODEON acoustic simulation software to model a small square in Turin, Italy. The researchers changed facade properties, especially absorption and scattering coefficients, using a pool of typical building facade materials. They then created auralizations, meaning audible simulations of the modeled acoustic scenes, and used listening tests to examine how people perceived the space.

The central finding is that facade absorption can influence subjective perception of spatial wideness. The source claim reports ANOVA results for two listener subgroups, with p-values below the selected significance level for both listener positions. The broader input also says that multiple regression analysis was used to relate perceived wideness to objective acoustic parameters, and that the relation depended strongly on listening position. That is a subtle but important result: the same urban square may not sound spatially identical from every point inside it.

The study matters because it moves beyond loudness alone. Urban sound is often discussed as noise level, but perception includes reverberation, clarity, spaciousness, and spatial impression. Facade materials and geometry influence reflections, scattering, and absorption. A reflective facade can return sound into the square, while a more absorptive or scattering surface can change the timing and strength of reflections. Those physical changes can alter what listeners think the space is like.

The evidence is moderate. The controlled simulation makes it possible to isolate facade properties in a way that would be difficult in a real city, where traffic, weather, crowds, and visual impressions vary. The listening-test sample of 31 gives a measured human response rather than relying only on acoustic prediction. However, the study remains bounded by one modeled square, simulated scenes, headphone or listening-test presentation, and the selected materials and positions. Real streets include multisensory context, movement, social activity, and changing sound sources.

For designers, the implication is direct: facade design is also acoustic design. Material selection and surface articulation can shape the public realm, not merely the building envelope. The result supports including acoustic perception earlier in urban design decisions, especially for squares and street canyons. It does not provide a universal formula for all cities, but it shows that facade choices can change how urban space is heard and interpreted.
cards.apa_citation: Evaluation of the influence of building facade design on the acoustic characteristics and auditory perception of urban spaces.

cards.title: Neuroarchitecture Has Signals, But Not Yet Certainty
cards.body: Built environments can be studied through brain activity, but the field is still young and uneven. This critical review found that lighting, layout, views, natural settings, and interior form have all been linked to measured neural responses. Yet the methods differ widely. The result is not one settled rule for design, but a map of a field learning how to measure the mind indoors.
cards.expanded_summary: The review began with more than 150 records and retained 87 related papers, including 52 original studies on built-environment effects and brain activity. EEG was common because it is comparatively portable, while fMRI was less common in building contexts. The literature suggests that natural environments often align with calmer or more meditative brain states, while some built settings align with stress or vigilance. But heterogeneous tasks, settings, measures, and samples prevent a single pooled conclusion. The review is best read as a disciplined field diagnosis.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through EEG, fMRI.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card summarizes a critical review on how built environments affect measured brain activity. The review examined research connecting environmental features to momentary states of mind, with special attention to neural measures such as EEG and fMRI. The input says the authors began with more than 150 records, retained 87 relevant papers, and identified 52 original studies directly examining built-environment effects on users' brain activity.

The central finding is that the field has real signals but uneven evidence. Studies suggest that natural environments are often associated with more disentangled, calmer, or meditative brain activity, while built environments have sometimes been associated with higher stress, frustration, or vigilance. The review also highlights design features such as lighting, spatial arrangement, views, and interior curvature. One conclusion in the input says that curvature in interior design was identified as encouraging engaging brain activity that reflects attraction to surroundings. The source claim refers to work using EEG methods to study human-building interaction under different indoor temperatures, showing the breadth of variables being considered.

The review's importance lies partly in method. EEG appears often because it is portable, less expensive, and easier to use near real or simulated environments. fMRI can offer different kinds of neural detail, but it is harder to deploy in realistic building studies. This method mix shapes what the field can know. If most evidence comes from short exposures, lab settings, or specific tasks, the conclusions will naturally emphasize immediate brain responses rather than long-term wellbeing.

The evidence should be treated as moderate and heterogeneous. A critical review can show patterns and gaps, but it cannot turn inconsistent primary studies into one precise causal estimate. The input notes variation in setting, task, exposure length, population, and neural measure. Many studies may be small or narrow. Publication bias may also favor striking positive findings. The review does not provide a pooled effect size, so it cannot say exactly how large each design effect is.

For practice, the lesson is careful rather than grand. Designers should treat lighting, layout, views, temperature, and form as possible contributors to neural state, but they should not claim that one feature automatically produces one brain outcome. The review points toward better reporting: define the environmental stimulus, measure exposure clearly, describe the task, and use comparable neural outcomes. Neuroarchitecture becomes more useful when it is precise about what was changed, who experienced it, and what was measured.
cards.apa_citation: A critical review on the impact of built environment on users' measured brain activity.

cards.title: Cedar Rooms Lowered Blood Pressure Despite Preference
cards.body: People's opinions about a room did not tell the whole physiological story. In this study, 83 subjects spent time in a room with planed Japanese cedar and a comparable room with printed grain resin boards. Subjective ratings followed personal preference, but blood pressure decreased in the cedar room regardless of preference. The body and the opinion did not move as one.
cards.expanded_summary: Each participant stayed in both rooms for 30 minutes while researchers measured blood pressure, salivary stress markers, mood, semantic differential ratings, and volatile organic compounds. The cedar room did not necessarily win every conscious preference judgment, but it reduced blood pressure compared with the artificial-material room. Salivary alpha-amylase was repressed in both rooms. The result is valuable because it separates subjective comfort from physiological response. Still, it comes from one material comparison, one setting, and a limited exposure period.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Survey, Questionnaire, Rating Task.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card is based on an empirical study comparing psychological and physiological responses to two interior material treatments. The study used 83 subjects and two rooms. One room used planed Japanese cedar lumber. The other used printed grain resin sheet overlay boards. Participants stayed in each room for 30 minutes. The researchers measured subjective evaluations, mood, salivary stress markers, blood pressure, pulse-related measures, and volatile organic compounds in the rooms.

The main result is a separation between preference and physiology. Subjective evaluations were strongly influenced by preference. People tended to rate the room they liked more positively. This is not surprising, but it is important: conscious judgment matters in interior experience. Yet the physiological responses did not simply follow preference. The input reports that blood pressure decreased in the Japanese cedar room compared with the artificial-material room, regardless of which room participants preferred. Salivary alpha-amylase activity was repressed in both rooms.

The source claim adds a specific blood-pressure detail: analysis of systolic blood pressure showed that 76.0 percent of the 83 subjects had decreased systolic blood pressure by an average of 7 mmHg after staying in the rooms, with data described across preference groups. The broader input emphasizes that the cedar room reduced blood pressure compared with the room using artificial materials. The evidence therefore supports a moderate claim that material treatment can affect physiological response independently of explicit liking.

This matters for design because interiors are often evaluated by appearance, preference, or market taste. Those are real outcomes, but they may not capture how the body responds. A finish that is liked is not automatically the same as a finish that lowers physiological arousal. Conversely, a material may produce a beneficial physiological pattern even among people who do not strongly prefer it.

The limits are clear. The study compared one natural material treatment with one artificial material treatment in a specific setting. The exposure lasted 30 minutes, and the population and context may not represent all users. The design cannot answer whether all woods have the same effect, whether the effect persists over months, or whether visual, olfactory, tactile, or cultural cues drove the response. For practice, the finding supports testing interior materials with both subjective and physiological measures before treating preference as the final answer.
cards.apa_citation: Effects and interaction of different interior material treatment and personal preference on psychological and physiological responses in living environment.

cards.title: Room Preference Did Not Predict Every Body Response
cards.body: A room can be liked for one reason and affect the body for another. In the cedar-room study, participants' subjective evaluations depended strongly on which room they preferred. Yet physiological response was less obedient to preference: blood pressure decreased in the Japanese cedar room compared with the artificial-material room. That split makes interior materials more than a matter of taste.
cards.expanded_summary: The study compared planed Japanese cedar lumber with printed grain resin boards in two otherwise comparable rooms. Participants spent 30 minutes in each room, then contributed subjective ratings, mood responses, blood pressure measures, salivary stress markers, and other physiological data. Figure-based results tracked changes before entering and after exiting each room. The important point is not that preference is irrelevant. It is that preference and physiology can diverge, so design evaluation should not collapse bodily response into liking scores.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Survey, Questionnaire, Rating Task.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card uses the same empirical study as the related cedar-room card, but emphasizes the figure-based comparison of blood pressure and pulse-rate changes before and after room exposure. The study compared two interior material treatments: planed Japanese cedar lumber and printed grain resin sheet overlay boards. Eighty-three participants spent 30 minutes in each room. The researchers collected subjective evaluations, mood measures, salivary stress markers, blood pressure, pulse-related measures, and room volatile organic compound data.

The source claim refers to a figure showing variation in blood pressure and pulse rate between rooms at pre-entering and post-exiting measurements for preference groups. That detail matters because the study did not rely only on a single after-the-fact rating. It tracked bodily measures around exposure to each room. The broader input reports that subjective evaluations were strongly dependent on preference, while physiological responses were not affected by preference in the same way. The Japanese cedar room reduced blood pressure compared with the artificial-material room.

The conceptual value is the separation of two common design outcomes. One outcome is what people say they like, or how they describe the room. The other is what happens in measured physiological response. The study found that preference influenced subjective evaluation: people judged their preferred room more positively. But blood pressure reduction in the cedar condition was not simply a reflection of liking. This makes the study useful for evidence-based interior design because it warns against using preference surveys as a complete proxy for wellbeing.

The evidence is moderate. A sample of 83 is meaningful for this type of interior-room comparison, and the two-room design provides a direct contrast. The 30-minute exposure is long enough to observe short-term changes, but it is not a long-term occupancy study. The comparison is also specific: Japanese cedar versus printed grain resin sheet overlay boards. The result should not be generalized to all natural materials, all woods, all artificial materials, or all residential settings without further tests.

For practice, the prudent implication is to evaluate materials through several lenses. Preference data can reveal comfort, identity, familiarity, and cultural taste. Physiological data can reveal responses that occupants may not notice or report accurately. When those two data streams agree, the case for a material becomes stronger. When they diverge, as they partly did here, designers should resist simple conclusions and ask which outcome matters for the intended space.
cards.apa_citation: Effects and interaction of different interior material treatment and personal preference on psychological and physiological responses in living environment.

cards.title: Wood Surfaces Speak Through Several Senses
cards.body: Wooden interiors are not only seen. They can also be heard, smelled, and touched, and this review argues that those material properties may shape psychological and physiological response. The evidence is not complete enough for firm prescriptions. Its useful contribution is a map: color, grain, knots, acoustics, odor, and texture are all possible sensory routes through which wood may affect people.
cards.expanded_summary: The literature review examined material properties of wooden interior surfaces and their connection to sensory stimulation. It considered visual, auditory, olfactory, and tactile pathways, along with possible responses in the brain, autonomic nervous system, endocrine system, and immune system. The review found valuable prior work but also a lack of precise knowledge about which wood properties matter, how strongly they matter, and under what conditions. Designers can use the framework cautiously, while waiting for stronger empirical tests of material dose and response.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Rating Task.
cards.short_science_summary:
This card summarizes a narrative literature review on wooden interior surfaces, material properties, sensory stimulation, and human response. The review asks which properties of wood are associated with sensory stimulation, and which recommendations or requirements might guide the use of wood on interior walls, ceilings, and floors. The paper considers psychological responses, such as emotional reactions to interiors, and physiological responses, including possible changes in brain activity, the autonomic nervous system, the endocrine system, and the immune system.

The important feature of the review is its multisensory scope. Wood is not treated only as a visual finish. The input says the paper discusses visual, olfactory, auditory, and tactile stimulation from wooden surfaces. Visual properties may include grain, color, knots, and surface variation. Auditory properties may include how wood affects sound in a room, and the source claim mentions studies analyzing effects of various sounds on heart rate, skin conductance, and respiratory rate in a building with wooden flooring. Olfactory properties may involve odors from the material. Tactile properties may involve surface feel and contact.

The review's conclusion is cautious. Earlier studies contain valuable information, but questions remain about which exact material properties of wood are responsible for observed effects and how strong those effects are. The input explicitly states that the results show a lack of knowledge regarding material properties and the degree to which they affect sensory stimulation. That makes this paper more of a framework and evidence map than a definitive intervention trial.

The evidence is moderate because a literature review can organize mechanisms and design considerations, but it cannot replace consistent empirical measurement. The review has one table and many sections, which suggests a structured discussion, but it does not provide one pooled causal estimate. The primary studies likely vary in population, setting, wood type, exposure length, and outcome measure. Those differences make broad claims risky.

For design, the review recommends thinking about wood as an active sensory material rather than a neutral surface. Covering walls, ceilings, or floors with wood may alter indoor quality and human response, but the best design choice depends on which sensory pathway matters in the specific space. A calm bedroom, a classroom, a workplace, and a healthcare waiting room may require different material properties. The practical next step is better measurement: specify the wood, the sensory exposure, the room context, and the human outcome before claiming a benefit.
cards.apa_citation: Material properties of wooden surfaces used in interiors and sensory stimulation.

cards.title: Architecture Needs a Shared Brain Vocabulary
cards.body: Neuroscience has begun to give architects new ways to study how buildings feel, but the field is still young. This review found that research clusters around restoration, stress, aesthetics, experience, and measurement methods. Its main lesson is sober: brain-based design cannot advance by slogans. It needs shared terms, clear methods, and careful boundaries.
cards.expanded_summary: The review maps the meeting point between architecture and neuroscience rather than claiming one universal effect of buildings on the brain. It separates older theories, experimental studies, and newer techniques such as EEG, eye tracking, electrodermal activity, questionnaires, and post-test measures. That structure is useful because it shows why different studies sometimes talk past one another. A corridor, facade, garden, or room can be studied as a perceptual, emotional, or physiological event, but only if researchers say exactly which response they mean and how they measured it.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through EEG, EYE-TRACKING.
cards.short_science_summary:
This systematic qualitative review asks what neuroscience currently contributes to the study of human experience in the built environment. Its answer is not a single design rule. The review argues that the field is forming around several related but distinct strands: restorative and stress-reducing environmental effects, aesthetic judgment and appreciation, phenomenological experience, user experience, and the methods used to measure these responses. That is why the paper is best read as a map of a developing research area rather than as evidence for one particular material, room shape, or design feature.

The review searched English-language sources from 2000 onward using neuroscience-and-architecture keywords.
