It then organized the literature into precursor theories, neuroscience studies, and emerging concepts. The methods mentioned in the input include EEG, eye tracking, electrodermal activity, questionnaires, semantic design disciplines, and post-test measures. These tools do different things. EEG can track electrical brain activity, eye tracking can show visual attention, electrodermal activity can index arousal, and questionnaires can capture conscious appraisal. None of these alone explains the whole human experience of a building.

The central claim is methodological. Architecture and neuroscience need a shared vocabulary before they can make stronger claims about built space. Without common terms, one study may describe relaxation, another attention, another aesthetic appreciation, and another stress, while all appear to be studying the same environment. The review's value is that it exposes this problem and gives the field a more ordered structure. It also warns against treating brain language as decorative authority. A neural or physiological measure is useful only when the construct is named clearly and the interpretation stays close to what was measured.

For practice, the review suggests caution as well as promise. Built environments can plausibly influence stress, attention, appreciation, interest, focus, pleasure, anxiety, connectedness, and related experiences. But the strength of any claim depends on the study design, the population, the measured response, and the way the environment was described. The evidence is moderate because it synthesizes a field and identifies recurring concepts, but it does not provide one decisive experiment. The bounded search strategy also matters: English-language, keyword-driven searching from 2000 onward may miss work using different labels or published outside that frame. The safest conclusion is that neuroscience can sharpen design research when its terms and measures are explicit.
cards.apa_citation: Exploring the influence of the built environment on human experience through a neuroscience approach: A systematic review.

cards.title: Crowds Stress Walkers Most When They Slow Them
cards.body: Crowding is not automatically stressful in the same way everywhere. This work found a sharper pattern: density alone did not reliably raise objective stress, and speed alone did not do so either. Physiological arousal rose when density reduced walking speed. A crowded station feels worse when it blocks your movement, not merely when it contains many people.
cards.expanded_summary: The study matters because it separates two things people often combine: being near others and being forced to move differently. Across standing, waiting, and walking situations, subjective ratings and electrodermal measures did not always tell the same story. Participants could rate dense conditions as unpleasant without showing the same physiological rise. The strongest support for pedestrian level-of-service assumptions came when people were moving and crowding constrained their speed. That makes crowd design a question of flow, control, and thresholds, not just head counts per square meter.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through electrodermal, skin conductance.
cards.short_science_summary:
This research examines how pedestrian density, walking speed, and psychological stress relate in crowded transport-like settings. Its main finding is more precise than the familiar claim that crowds are stressful. Density by itself did not reliably increase objective physiological stress across all conditions. Walking speed by itself also did not directly raise physiological stress when considered separately. The important result appeared when density restricted walking speed. Under those conditions, physiological arousal increased, supporting the idea that crowding becomes more stressful when it interferes with movement.

The work used both subjective and objective stress measures. Subjective ratings captured how unpleasant participants found different conditions. Electrodermal activity, including skin conductance level and non-specific skin conductance responses, gave a physiological measure related to sympathetic arousal. This distinction is important. People may report discomfort in a dense situation even when physiological measures do not rise in the same way. Conversely, a bodily arousal signal can reveal that a situation is placing demands on the person beyond conscious preference.

The research spans four studies. Some examined standing or waiting in dense situations. Others examined walking at preset or freely chosen speeds. A later study combined density and walking speed to test the relation more directly. The density conditions mentioned in the input range from 2 to 8 persons per square meter, and the reported statistical surface includes p values and effect-size language. One result notes differences in skin conductance level and non-specific skin conductance responses, while another notes a main effect of density on skin conductance in a waiting condition. The complete interpretation, however, should not reduce the whole thesis to one simple crowding law.

For design and planning, the implication is practical. Pedestrian comfort should not be judged only by the number of bodies in a space. Designers also need to ask whether the density prevents people from walking at a tolerable pace, choosing a route, and avoiding unwanted contact. The evidence is moderate because it includes multiple studies and physiological measurement, but its scope remains bounded by the experimental and quasi-naturalistic conditions used. The safest claim is that crowd stress is conditional: it rises most clearly when density limits movement and control.
cards.apa_citation: The Relationship between Pedestrian Density, Walking Speed and Psychological Stress: Examining Physiological Arousal in Crowded Situations.

cards.title: City Streets Become Emotional When Space Is Crossed
cards.body: A busy street is not just a transport channel. In this Hong Kong walking study, pedestrians carried wearable sensors while moving through crowded urban and park segments. The key idea is that proximity matters. When other pedestrians crossed into personal space, the body showed emotional responses, linking street density with stress-related arousal in real motion.
cards.expanded_summary: The study used a predefined route rather than a still photograph or laboratory crowd. Thirty participants walked through a commercial center that included several street paths and park sections. Wearable devices tracked electrodermal activity, skin temperature, ambient temperature, and movement, while proximity sensing estimated personal-space crossings. The result gives designers a more human description of crowding: emotional load depends on how often the path forces close encounters, not just how dense the district looks on a plan.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred by having pedestrians walk a predefined urban route while wearable sensors recorded electrodermal activity, skin temperature, ambient temperature, movement, and proximity-based personal-space crossings.
cards.short_science_summary:
This empirical study examined how crowding density in a high-density urban environment relates to pedestrians' emotional responses while they are actually walking. The setting was a Hong Kong commercial center with urban parts and a park. Thirty subjects walked a predefined route, and wearable devices recorded psychophysiological signals during the walk. The study is therefore useful because it moves the crowding question out of a purely abstract or static setting and into a moving street context.

The central idea is personal space. The authors hypothesized that high density can invade the personal space surrounding each person's body and that these invasions can evoke emotional responses, especially negative emotions such as stress or aggression. The measurement system captured electrodermal activity, skin temperature, ambient temperature, and movement-related information. A movement detection sensor was used to measure personal-space crossings by other pedestrians. The input also notes that walking times differed between street paths, with one path taking the most time and another the least, which reinforces that the route itself mattered.

The main finding is that personal-space crossings had significant effects on aversive and appetitive emotional responses. This does not mean every crowded street will produce the same response in every pedestrian. It means that, in this route and sample, close encounters were linked to measurable emotional changes. Electrodermal activity is especially relevant because it is commonly used as an index of bodily arousal. Skin temperature and ambient temperature add useful context, since outdoor and semi-outdoor walking environments vary in more than social density.

For urban design, the practical lesson is that density should not be treated as background noise. A route may feel stressful because it repeatedly compresses interpersonal distance, slows movement, or gives pedestrians few opportunities to adjust their path. Parks, commercial edges, sidewalk widths, crossings, and bottlenecks can all alter how often people enter one another's personal space. The evidence is moderate because the study uses real walking and wearable sensing, but it is limited by a small sample, one city, and one route. The safest interpretation is local and conditional: in dense pedestrian settings, repeated proximity intrusions can become part of the emotional burden of the street.
cards.apa_citation: Crowding density in urban environment and its effects on emotional responding of pedestrians: Using wearable device technology with sensors capturing proximity and psychophysiological emotion responses while walking in the street.

cards.title: Mindful Buildings Depend on More Than Quiet
cards.body: Architecture may support attention, but this review does not reduce mindfulness to silence. Across 32 articles, recurring design qualities included natural forms, enclosure, natural light, uniform lighting, acoustics, air-related comfort, natural materials, gardens, vegetation, brick, and green color. The strongest message is atmospheric: attention is helped when several environmental cues work together.
cards.expanded_summary: The review used a systematic search, content analysis, and word-frequency association to turn a scattered literature into a design vocabulary. It found no direct field devoted only to enhancing mindfulness through architecture, so it built its claim from attention-supporting evidence. That makes the conclusion useful but bounded. Natural material, view, and color aligned especially well with biophilic design, while sound, light, enclosure, facade, entrance, and object elements formed part of the same attention-supportive pattern. The emphasis is on converging cues, not a single calming ingredient.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Review Article.
cards.short_science_summary:
This review asks how architectural design strategies may promote attention in ways that could foster mindfulness. The paper treats attention as a central vehicle of mindfulness, but it also notes a research gap: architecture has not yet been directly studied as a mature field of mindfulness enhancement. For that reason, the article should be read as a synthesis of attention-supporting architectural qualities rather than as proof that any single design feature reliably produces mindfulness.

The study used three stages. First, it searched Scopus in February 2024 for publications related to architecture that promotes attention. After screening for suitability and accessibility, 32 articles were included. Second, three coders independently applied a framework developed from prior literature, and the main researcher compiled the final dataset. Third, the authors used Python-based analysis of word frequency and association to identify which design elements appeared most strongly in the selected literature.

The review found that architectural atmosphere was central. Natural forms and spaces that evoke enclosure were prominent. Lighting qualities included natural light and uniformity. Sound design appeared through acoustics, ambient sound, and noise control. Weather and air aspects were also part of the pattern. Building features included natural materials, object elements, facades, entrances, views, gardens, vegetation, brick color, and green color. Material, view, and color were especially congruent with biophilic design. One article discussed mindfulness through alpha brainwave activity, while another treated mindfulness as a silent condition, which shows how varied the underlying definitions can be.

For design practice, the most useful conclusion is that attention-supportive architecture is probably multi-cue. A room is unlikely to support calm attention through one isolated choice if other cues are disruptive. Natural light, acoustic control, coherent enclosure, natural materials, and views may work together to shape the atmosphere in which attention becomes easier. The evidence is moderate because the review is systematic and coded, but the analysis is based on a limited article corpus, a particular search database and month, and word association rather than direct intervention. The safest claim is that the review identifies recurring design strategies associated with attention and plausibly relevant to mindfulness, not guaranteed prescriptions.
cards.apa_citation: The Architectural Design Strategies That Promote Attention to Foster Mindfulness: A Systematic Review, Content Analysis and Meta-Analysis.

cards.title: Office Satisfaction Starts With Space, Noise, and Privacy
cards.body: In a large office survey, the strongest predictors of workspace satisfaction were not only temperature or air quality. Across 52,980 occupants in 351 mostly US office buildings, satisfaction with amount of space ranked first, followed by noise level and visual privacy. Strikingly, perceived space mattered more than the gross amount of space per person.
cards.expanded_summary: The study used ten years of Center for the Built Environment survey data and 7-point satisfaction scales. A proportional odds ordinal logistic regression found that all 15 surveyed parameters contributed significantly to overall workspace satisfaction. Yet the hierarchy was clear: amount of space had the highest odds ratio, then noise, then visual privacy. The result held across age group, gender, office type, window distance, and satisfaction group, making perceived spatial adequacy a unusually robust office-design variable.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Survey, Rating Task.
cards.short_science_summary:
This study examined which indoor environmental quality factors and building features most affect occupant satisfaction in mainly US office buildings. Its evidence base is unusually large: 52,980 occupant responses from 351 office buildings collected over ten years through the Center for the Built Environment web-based occupant satisfaction survey. Most respondents worked in air-conditioned buildings without LEED ratings, according to the input. The survey used 7-point ordered-scale questions about satisfaction with indoor environmental parameters, workspace features, and building features.

The main statistical method was proportional odds ordinal logistic regression. The paper found that satisfaction with all 15 surveyed parameters contributed significantly to overall workspace satisfaction. However, the most important parameters were satisfaction with amount of space, noise level, and visual privacy. The reported odds ratios were 1.57 for amount of space, 1.27 for noise level, and 1.26 for visual privacy, with narrow confidence intervals. Satisfaction with amount of space was ranked as the strongest influence regardless of age group, gender, type of office, distance from a window, or whether the worker was generally satisfied or dissatisfied with the workspace.

One important nuance is that satisfaction with amount of space was not related to the gross amount of space available per person. That means the psychological experience of space cannot be reduced to square footage alone. Layout, storage, enclosure, furniture arrangement, visual crowding, and the ability to work without interference may all affect whether a person feels they have enough space, even when the formal area measure is similar.

For practice, the evidence points toward concrete priorities. Designers and facility managers should invest in features that improve perceived space, storage, acoustic conditions, and visual privacy. The input also says office workers were most satisfied when close to a window in a private office, but that should be read as part of the survey pattern, not as a universal mandate. The evidence is moderate to strong for association because the sample is large and the model compares many variables. It remains survey-based, however, so it shows which satisfactions move together rather than proving that changing one feature will always cause a proportional change in satisfaction. The safest conclusion is that perceived spatial adequacy, noise control, and privacy are central to office satisfaction.
cards.apa_citation: IEQ aspects of 351 office buildings (N=52,980 occupant responses): space, noise, visual privacy, temperature, air quali....

cards.title: Wood Knots May Dull Some Comfort Benefits
cards.body: Wood is often treated as naturally calming, but this experiment adds a useful qualification. Twenty-eight Japanese female university students viewed computer-generated wooden wall images with no knots, few knots, or several knots. The physiological measures did not differ significantly, while all images promoted some comfort and relaxation. Several knots reduced the psychological benefit compared with clear wood.
cards.expanded_summary: The study measured oxyhemoglobin concentrations in the left and right prefrontal cortex and heart rate variability while participants viewed each wooden-wall image for 90 seconds. It also used psychological rating tools, including a modified semantic differential method and the Profile of Mood State. The result is not that knots are harmful in a sweeping sense. It is narrower: in short visual exposure, clear wood and wood with few knots looked psychologically better than wood with several knots, while physiology stayed statistically unchanged.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through heart rate.
cards.short_science_summary:
This empirical study investigated whether the visible knot pattern on wooden wall surfaces changes psychological and physiological responses. Wood is widely used in interior decoration, and knots are common features of wood surfaces. They are often considered surface defects because they can reduce mechanical properties and aesthetic quality. The study asks whether knotty wood also reduces the psychological or physiological benefits often associated with wooden interiors.

The participants were 28 adult Japanese female university students. They viewed three computer-generated full-sized wooden-wall images: one clear wooden wall, one wooden wall with few knots, and one wooden wall with several knots. Each image was viewed for 90 seconds in random order. During exposure, the researchers measured oxyhemoglobin concentrations in the left and right prefrontal cortex as indicators of central nervous system activity. They also measured heart rate variability as an indicator of autonomic nervous system activity. Psychological responses were assessed with a modified semantic differential method and the Profile of Mood State, 2nd edition.

The main result is carefully bounded. There was no significant difference among the three images in physiological responses. That means the study did not show that knot amount changed prefrontal oxygenation or heart rate variability during this brief viewing task. Psychologically, however, all stimuli promoted some degree of comfort and relaxation, and the image with several knots was associated with reduced psychological benefits compared with the clear wooden-wall image. The image with few knots did not differ remarkably from the clear image.

For interior design, the lesson is neither that all wood is calming nor that knots should always be avoided. The better conclusion is that visible surface complexity may affect conscious comfort even when short-term physiological measures do not separate the conditions. Clear wood and lightly knotty wood may preserve the perceived comfort often associated with wooden surfaces, while heavily knotty visual patterns may weaken it for some viewers.

The evidence is moderate but limited. The study used images rather than real walls, passive viewing rather than occupancy, a short 90-second exposure, and a narrow participant group. Its null physiological finding is important because it prevents overclaiming. The safest interpretation is that knot amount can influence psychological appraisal of wood surfaces under these viewing conditions, while physiological effects remain unconfirmed in this experiment.

cards.title: Classroom Comfort Can Track Student Anxiety
cards.body: A teaching building is not just a container for learning. In this survey of 440 students, perceived ventilation, lighting, noise, courtyard thermal comfort, and semi-outdoor views were significantly linked to anxiety likelihood. Better physical conditions corresponded with lower anxiety risk, making classroom design part of mental well-being rather than mere convenience.
cards.expanded_summary: The study focused on a specific teaching building at Zhejiang Sci-Tech University and examined interior, courtyard, and semi-outdoor areas. It used questionnaires, GAD-7 anxiety measurement, correlation analysis, covariance checks, multiple linear regression, and binary logistic regression. The strongest message is practical: students' mental well-being relates to several linked environmental perceptions. Poor corridor ventilation, unsuitable classroom lighting, noise, thermal discomfort, and weak outdoor connection are not separate annoyances. They form the conditions in which students judge and inhabit academic space.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Survey, Questionnaire.
cards.short_science_summary:
This study examined how students' perceptions of physical environment factors in teaching buildings relate to mental well-being, especially anxiety likelihood. The input describes it as using data from 440 valid questionnaires, after an initial survey of 465 students. The setting was Building 2 at Zhejiang Sci-Tech University, with data collected in April 2023. The study considered interior classrooms, courtyards, and semi-outdoor areas, rather than treating the teaching building as a single undifferentiated space.

The measurement approach was survey-based. Students reported their perceptions of environmental factors, and anxiety was assessed with GAD-7. The authors used descriptive statistics, correlation matrices, covariance checks, multiple linear regression, and two binary logistic regression models. This design can identify strong associations between perceived environmental quality and anxiety likelihood, but it cannot by itself prove that changing a building feature would cause a specific reduction in anxiety.

The reported findings are nevertheless clear. Students' perceptions of classroom ventilation, lighting, noise conditions, courtyard thermal comfort, and views from semi-outdoor areas significantly influenced the likelihood of experiencing anxiety. The input gives several p values below 0.01 and odds ratios, including OR = 0.330 for classroom ventilation, OR = 0.444 for lighting, OR = 0.415 for noise conditions, OR = 0.504 for courtyard thermal comfort, and OR = 2.779 for views from semi-outdoor areas. The broad interpretation is that optimal physical conditions were linked with reduced student anxiety.

The paper also points to specific design trouble spots. It notes problems related to unsuitable lighting in classroom window areas and issues with ventilation, lighting, and noise caused by corridor layout. These details make the result useful for renovation as well as new design. Facilities teams can treat ventilation, lighting uniformity, acoustic control, courtyard thermal comfort, and semi-outdoor views as mental-well-being variables.

The evidence is moderate. It has a decent student sample and a concrete building context, but it remains cross-sectional and self-reported. It measures perceived environment and reported anxiety, not direct physiology or longitudinal change after renovation. The safest claim is that students' assessment of teaching-building conditions is meaningfully associated with anxiety likelihood, and that better physical environments are plausible targets for mental-health-supportive design.
cards.apa_citation: A Perceptual Assessment of the Physical Environment in Teaching Buildings and Its Influence on Students' Mental Well-Being.

cards.title: Bright Cool Light Wakes People Before It Comforts Them
cards.body: Abrupt light changes affect people quickly, but not all responses move together. In this controlled experiment, transitions between warm dim and cool bright lighting shifted perceived brightness, color temperature, alertness, vitality, and comfort. Alertness rose rapidly and persisted, while visual comfort and acceptance could dip temporarily, especially when moving toward brighter, cooler light.
cards.expanded_summary: Thirty-eight healthy adults participated in four 90-minute sessions with different lighting trajectories: warm dim to cool bright, cool bright to warm dim, constant warm dim, and constant cool bright. Subjective visual experience changed clearly. Objective physiological measures such as heart rate, heart rate variability, skin conductance, skin temperature, and distal-to-proximate gradient changed little, except for a gradual skin conductance increase in constant cool bright light. The asymmetry matters: turning light up is not simply the reverse of turning it down.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through HRV, Heart rate, skin conductance.
cards.short_science_summary:
This 2020 within-subjects laboratory experiment studied how abrupt lighting transitions affect alertness, arousal, and comfort. Thirty-eight healthy subjects took part in four separate 90-minute sessions at Eindhoven University of Technology. The lighting conditions contrasted warm dim lighting with cool bright lighting. The four trajectories were warm dim to cool bright, cool bright to warm dim, constant warm dim, and constant cool bright.

The study measured several kinds of response. Subjective measures included sensation of light intensity, sensation of color temperature, visual comfort, visual acceptance, alertness, and mood dimensions such as calmness, happiness, and vitality. Objective measures included psychomotor vigilance task performance, heart rate, heart rate variability, skin conductance level, skin temperature, and distal-to-proximate skin temperature gradient. Mixed models were used, with participant and session as random intercepts, and analyses compared transition and constant conditions across measurement blocks.

The main finding is that subjective visual and alertness responses were clearer than physiological arousal responses. Visual appraisals such as perceived brightness, perceived color temperature, comfort, and acceptance responded quickly to lighting changes. Alertness and vitality also responded immediately and persisted. However, comfort and visual acceptance could show a temporary dip that recovered within roughly 15 to 30 minutes. The input notes that fewer participants accepted constant cool bright light compared with constant warm dim light across blocks, which fits the broader pattern that brighter, cooler lighting can be activating but not always more comfortable.

Physiological measures did not strongly track abrupt transitions. Heart rate, heart rate variability, skin conductance, and skin temperature showed little response to the transition, with the notable exception of a gradual skin conductance increase in constant cool bright lighting that may indicate a slower activating pathway. This matters because designers should not assume that every perceived alertness shift reflects immediate broad physiological arousal.

For lighting design, the result supports dynamic lighting but argues for care in transition speed and direction. Cool bright light may rapidly support alertness, yet abrupt upward shifts can temporarily reduce comfort. Gradual ramping may preserve benefits while reducing disruption. The evidence is moderate: the design is controlled and within-subjects, but the sample is modest, daytime exposure may not generalize to evening, and the lighting contrasts were strong. The safest conclusion is that light transitions change experience quickly, while bodily arousal changes are weaker and more complex.
cards.apa_citation: M.E. Kompier, K.C.H.J. Smolders, W.D. van Marken Lichtenbelt, Y.A.W. de Kort (2020). Effects of light transitions on measures of alertness, arousal and comfort. https://doi.org/10.1016/j.physbeh.2020.112999

cards.title: Touching Oak Calmed the Body More Than Stone or Steel
cards.body: Materials do not only look different; they can feel different to the nervous system. In this experiment, 18 female university students touched uncoated white oak, marble, tile, and stainless steel for 90 seconds. White oak reduced prefrontal oxyhemoglobin more than the other materials and increased parasympathetic activity more than marble and stainless steel.
cards.expanded_summary: The participants sat with eyes closed, then touched each material with the palm. Near-infrared time-resolved spectroscopy measured oxyhemoglobin in the left and right prefrontal cortex, while heart rate variability indexed autonomic activity. The high-frequency component of HRV reflected parasympathetic activity, and the LF/HF ratio reflected sympathetic activity. The result suggests tactile wood contact can promote physiological relaxation, but the evidence is narrow: young female students, short exposure, and small material samples. It is a precise laboratory finding, not a general verdict on every wooden interior.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through HRV, Heart rate.
cards.short_science_summary:
This empirical study tested whether touching wood with the palm produces different physiological responses from touching other common interior materials. The participants were 18 female university students with a mean age of about 21.7 years. Smokers, people currently being treated for disease, and participants in their menstrual period during the study period were excluded. The tactile stimuli were plates of uncoated white oak, marble, tile, and stainless steel.

The procedure was simple and controlled. After sitting at rest with their eyes closed, participants touched each material for 90 seconds. Brain activity was measured through oxyhemoglobin concentrations in the left and right prefrontal cortex using near-infrared time-resolved spectroscopy. Autonomic nervous activity was measured with heart rate variability. The high-frequency component of heart rate variability was used as an indicator of parasympathetic activity, while the low-frequency to high-frequency ratio was used as an indicator of sympathetic activity.

The main finding is that tactile stimulation with white oak produced a more relaxed physiological pattern than the comparison materials. White oak significantly decreased oxyhemoglobin concentration in the left and right prefrontal cortex relative to marble, tile, and stainless steel. The source excerpt gives one left prefrontal comparison: white oak at -0.37, marble at -0.18, tile at -0.12, and stainless steel at 0.11. White oak also increased high-frequency heart rate variability, reflecting parasympathetic activity, relative to marble and stainless steel. The authors interpreted this as evidence that touching wood calms prefrontal cortex activity and induces parasympathetic nervous activity.

For design, this finding makes tactile material choice more than a visual or symbolic decision. Handrails, desks, wall panels, counters, and furniture surfaces may be part of the bodily experience of a room. A wooden surface could plausibly support relaxation during brief touch in a way that harder, cooler, or less organic materials do not. Still, the evidence should not be overstated. The sample was small and narrow, the exposure lasted only 90 seconds, and the study tested material plates rather than whole interiors. It also compared one wood species and finish with three other materials. The safest conclusion is that, under controlled short-touch conditions, uncoated white oak produced measurable signs of physiological relaxation compared with marble, tile, and stainless steel.
cards.apa_citation: Physiological Effects of Touching Wood.

cards.title: Natural-Looking Architecture Has Measurable Visual Ingredients
cards.body: A building can feel natural without being covered in plants. Across three experiments, this study found that low-level visual features, especially scaling and contrast, helped explain naturalness judgments, similarity judgments, and preference ratings for architectural scenes. People seemed to prefer some buildings because their patterns quietly echoed the statistical structure of natural environments.
cards.expanded_summary: The work used 240 architectural images, including interiors and exteriors, and combined human ratings with computational image analysis. One experiment tested naturalness ratings, another used an image arrangement task and multidimensional scaling, and a third tested aesthetic preference. Naturalness explained much of one similarity dimension, and scaling and contrast predicted preference partly through perceived naturalness. The result gives biophilic design a measurable route: not only plants and views, but pattern, color, and visual structure. Its scope remains image-based, so real buildings still require testing.
cards.measurables: Measured or inferred through Survey, Rating Task.
cards.short_science_summary:
This empirical paper asks whether subjective impressions of naturalness in architecture are driven by measurable visual patterns, and whether those patterns help explain similarity judgments and aesthetic preference. The core finding is that architectural naturalness is not only an opinion or style label. It can be partly predicted from low-level properties of images, especially scaling and contrast. Those features also matter for preference because they appear to shape latent perceptions of naturalness.

The research used three experiments. Experiment 1 recruited 100 American adults through MTurk and split them across interior and exterior architectural image sets. It tested whether spatial and color features could explain naturalness ratings. The input says low-level spatial and color features explained over half the variance in scene naturalness ratings, with scaling and contrast especially strong predictors for both interior and exterior scenes.

Experiment 2 recruited 167 participants for an image arrangement task. The resulting similarity data were analyzed with multidimensional scaling. Naturalness ratings explained over half the variance in one major similarity dimension, suggesting that people grouped architectural scenes partly according to an underlying sense of naturalness, even when the task was not simply to rate naturalness.

Experiment 3 recruited another 100 adults to rate aesthetic preference. Nature-like patterns of scaling and contrast significantly predicted preference ratings. The study also tested mediation using a bootstrap estimation approach with 10,000 samples. The effects of scaling and contrast on preference were mediated by latent perceptions of naturalness. In plain terms, people may prefer some architectural scenes partly because their measured visual structure evokes nature-like sensory qualities.

The stimulus pool included 240 architectural images, with 120 interiors and 120 exteriors, and feature sets measuring spatial properties, color properties, and explicit nature content. For design, the paper gives a useful bridge between biophilic intuition and measurable image structure. Designers need not rely only on literal vegetation. They can also tune geometry, contrast, color variation, and pattern scale to make built scenes feel more natural.

The evidence is moderate. It includes multiple experiments and computational measures, but it is based on image viewing and online tasks rather than actual building occupancy. The participant pools may not represent all cultures or user groups. The safest conclusion is that measurable visual features contribute to perceived naturalness and preference in architectural images, while real-world transfer still needs testing.
cards.apa_citation: PSYCHOLOGICAL RESPONSES TO NATURAL PATTERNS IN ARCHITECTURE.
